threats.title: onlyOperator Modifier Inverted Logic Permanently Blocks Designated Operators
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyOperator modifier uses `if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert ErrInvalidOperator()` which has inverted Boolean logic. For a designated operator where operator[addr]=true: the first condition `msg.sender != owner()` is true, so the OR evaluates to true and the function reverts. No designated operator can ever call harvest, delegateAmount, undelegateAmount, redelegateAmount, harvestAndDelegateRewards, or finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch. The correct logic should be `if (msg.sender != owner() && !operator[msg.sender])`. Only the owner who is NOT flagged as an operator can pass the modifier. The deployment script confirms this by never calling updateOperator and having the owner directly call delegateAmount.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect that prevents the core protocol from functioning as documented. The trust model states 'Operators (and Owner) can manage where assets are staked'. This modifier breaks that invariant completely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert — OR condition reverts when operator[msg.sender] is true, exactly the opposite of intent

preconditions: Owner calls updateOperator(botAddr, true) to designate an operator
Bot attempts to call any onlyOperator-gated function

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Boolean logic `A != B || C` always reverts when C is true, meaning operator[msg.sender]=true causes guaranteed revert
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: All staking management functions gated by broken modifier — protocol depends on owner-only execution
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — affects every call to any onlyOperator function by designated operators

operational_questions: Does any production deployment intend to use separate operator addresses?
If owner self-flags via updateOperator(owner, true), does this lock ALL operator functions with no caller able to pass?
validation_requirements: Deploy and attempt operator function calls from designated operator address — verify revert
Deploy and set operator[owner]=true, verify owner is also locked out
Confirm updateOperator(owner, false) restores owner access as recovery path

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: No attacker action needed — the bug is inherent in the modifier logic
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If owner sets operator[owner]=true (likely scenario for self-operation), all operator functions are locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User withdrawal requests via requestWithdrawal() accumulate but cannot be finalized
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Rewards cannot be harvested, delegations cannot be managed, withdrawal epochs cannot be finalized
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — this is a code defect, not an attack

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete loss of operator role delegation capability
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: If owner also locked out: no harvesting, no delegation, no withdrawal finalization — protocol frozen
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner can recover by calling updateOperator(owner, false), but designed operators can never function
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All staking management and withdrawal epoch finalization

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Immediate upon any operator attempting to use gated functions

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted operator function calls from designated operator addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: All operator functions only succeed from owner address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Operator role assignments that never result in successful operator calls

threats.title: harvestAndDelegateRewards Creates Unfunded Fee Obligation Draining Depositor Principal
threats.hypothesis_description: When harvestAndDelegateRewards is called, rewards are re-delegated to a validator via the Ronin staking contract — no WRON enters the vault. However, operatorFeeAmount is incremented by `(harvestedAmount * operatorFee) / BIPS`. When feeRecipient calls fetchOperatorFee(), it calls `_withdrawRONTo(feeRecipient, amount)` which unwraps WRON from the vault's balance. Since no WRON was deposited for these fees, the withdrawal drains depositor principal. Over repeated harvestAndDelegateRewards cycles, operatorFeeAmount can grow to exceed the vault's liquid WRON balance entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental accounting error where fees are tracked against non-existent vault funds. The Ronin staking rewards never enter the vault when re-delegated, yet the fee tracking assumes they did.

proof_of_signal.pattern: harvestAndDelegateRewards increments operatorFeeAmount but LiquidProxy.harvestAndDelegateRewards calls delegateRewards (no RON returns to vault). fetchOperatorFee at line 114-118 withdraws WRON from vault balance.

preconditions: At least one staking proxy deployed with active delegations earning rewards
Operator calls harvestAndDelegateRewards (re-delegates rewards)
feeRecipient calls fetchOperatorFee to claim accumulated fees

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: LiquidProxy.harvestAndDelegateRewards calls delegateRewards on staking contract (line 51-52), returning amount but no RON to vault. LiquidRon still increments operatorFeeAmount (line 141).
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Operator chooses harvestAndDelegateRewards over harvest, creating phantom fee each time
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every harvestAndDelegateRewards call creates additional unfunded fee obligation

operational_questions: How frequently will harvestAndDelegateRewards be called vs harvest in production?
Can operatorFeeAmount exceed vault WRON balance, causing fetchOperatorFee to revert?
What is the cumulative drain on depositor principal over time?
validation_requirements: Call harvestAndDelegateRewards, verify vault WRON balance unchanged but operatorFeeAmount increased
Call fetchOperatorFee, verify WRON withdrawn from vault equals depositor funds
Calculate maximum drain rate given expected reward rates and fee percentage

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Operator routinely uses harvestAndDelegateRewards for auto-compounding (intended workflow)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: operatorFeeAmount accumulates representing 2.5% of all re-delegated rewards
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: feeRecipient calls fetchOperatorFee, withdrawing WRON from depositor principal
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: totalAssets drops by the fee amount; depositors absorb the loss via decreased share price
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Normal protocol operation — no special coordination needed

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Fee mechanism systematically drains user deposits
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Vault insolvency if operatorFeeAmount exceeds liquid WRON
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Accumulated phantom fees cannot be reversed; depositors lose principal permanently
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All user deposits and withdrawals affected by reduced totalAssets

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None — automatic through standard protocol usage
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Cumulative — worsens with each harvestAndDelegateRewards call

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: fetchOperatorFee transactions reducing vault balance below expected depositor-backed amount
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated harvestAndDelegateRewards calls accumulating phantom obligations

threats.title: totalAssets Inflated by operatorFeeAmount Enabling Share Price Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: After harvest() is called, the full harvested WRON enters the vault balance and is counted in super.totalAssets(). The operator fee portion (operatorFeeAmount) is NOT subtracted from totalAssets(). This inflates the share price by the fee amount. When fetchOperatorFee() is later called, totalAssets drops by the fee amount, deflating the share price. Any user can observe harvest transactions on-chain (multi-block), redeem shares at the inflated price, wait for fetchOperatorFee, then re-deposit at the corrected (lower) price, extracting value from remaining depositors.
threats.investigation_rationale: The totalAssets override at line 253 adds super.totalAssets() + getTotalStaked() + getTotalRewards() but never subtracts operatorFeeAmount. After harvest, the fee sits in vault WRON balance inflating super.totalAssets().

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalAssets returns super.totalAssets() + getTotalStaked() + getTotalRewards() without subtracting operatorFeeAmount; operatorFeeAmount resides in vault WRON balance after harvest

preconditions: Operator has called harvest(), increasing vault WRON and operatorFeeAmount
fetchOperatorFee has not yet been called (fee sits in vault balance)
User can observe on-chain state after harvest execution

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: After harvest: totalAssets includes full harvestedAmount in vault WRON. getTotalRewards drops to 0. Net: totalAssets increases by operatorFee portion that should be excluded.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Timing between harvest and fetchOperatorFee creates exploitable window
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From harvest execution to fetchOperatorFee execution — could be hours or days

operational_questions: What is the typical time gap between harvest and fetchOperatorFee calls?
What is the maximum operatorFeeAmount relative to totalAssets?
Can the extraction be quantified per harvest cycle?
validation_requirements: Measure share price before and after harvest; compute inflation amount
Measure share price before and after fetchOperatorFee; compute deflation
Calculate extraction profit for an attacker with X shares over one cycle

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor on-chain for harvest() transaction execution (observable after block confirmation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Redeem existing shares at inflated share price (getting more assets per share)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait for fetchOperatorFee() to execute, deflating share price
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Deposit at corrected lower price, obtaining more shares than originally held
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: On-chain monitoring only — no mempool access needed

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None directly — this is an economic exploit
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Systematic value extraction from passive depositors
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be reversed; accumulated extraction is permanent
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All deposit/withdraw operations during the inflation window

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None — requires only LRON shares or WRON for deposits
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic on-chain monitoring and transaction execution
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Between harvest and fetchOperatorFee — observable window

threats.title: LiquidProxy.harvest() Claims Rewards N Times Redundantly and Uses Total Balance as Claimed Amount
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidProxy.harvest() iterates over _consensusAddrs.length and calls claimRewards(_consensusAddrs) (the FULL array) on each iteration, resulting in N redundant claim calls. The first call claims all rewards; subsequent calls either claim 0 or may revert depending on the Ronin staking contract's handling of double-claims. Additionally, the function uses `address(this).balance` as the claimed amount rather than measuring the delta. Any pre-existing native RON in the proxy (from the open receive() function or stuck funds from prior operations) is counted as harvested rewards, inflating operatorFeeAmount and distorting reward accounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop at line 38-40 of LiquidProxy.sol calls claimRewards with the full array each iteration. Combined with balance-based accounting at line 41, this creates both a gas waste / potential DoS and an accounting inaccuracy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for loop calls claimRewards(_consensusAddrs) N times with full array; then address(this).balance used as claimedAmount instead of delta measurement

preconditions: At least one staking proxy deployed with delegations to multiple validators
Proxy may have residual native RON from prior operations or direct sends
Operator calls harvest with _consensusAddrs array

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Loop calls claimRewards with entire array on each iteration (N*N complexity). If Ronin staking reverts on double-claim, harvest fails for multi-validator arrays.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Operator must call harvest — if it reverts due to double-claim, rewards are stuck
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any external party can send RON to proxy (open receive()) to inflate next harvest amount

operational_questions: Does Ronin staking's claimRewards revert on empty/double claims?
Can the redundant N calls cause gas limit exceedance for large validator sets?
Has any residual RON accumulated in proxies from prior undelegation/staking operations?
validation_requirements: Test harvest with 5+ consensus addresses — verify N redundant claimRewards calls
Send native RON to proxy, then call harvest — verify inflated claimedAmount
Check if Ronin staking reverts on second claimRewards call for same validators

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker sends small amount of native RON directly to a LiquidProxy address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: When operator calls harvest(), address(this).balance includes attacker's donation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Donated RON is sent to vault as WRON, operatorFeeAmount charged on full amount including donation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: If staking contract reverts on redundant claimRewards, harvest is DoS'd entirely
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single transaction to send RON to proxy address (public)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential harvest DoS if staking contract rejects redundant claims; inflated fee accounting
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Loop bug requires code fix; donated RON cannot be separated from legitimate rewards
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All reward harvesting via harvest() function

threats.title: ERC4626 First Depositor Inflation Attack Due to Zero decimalsOffset
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidRon does not override _decimalsOffset(), defaulting to 0. OpenZeppelin v5 ERC4626 uses virtual shares offset of 10^_decimalsOffset() = 1, providing minimal protection against the classic share inflation attack. An attacker can: (1) deposit 1 wei of WRON to mint 1 share, (2) donate a large amount of WRON directly to the vault contract (inflating totalAssets), (3) when a victim deposits, their deposit rounds down to 0 shares, and (4) the attacker redeems their 1 share for the entire vault balance. The deployment script seeds with 10000 ETH which would prevent this on first deployment, but this is deployment-dependent.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code has no _decimalsOffset override and no minimum deposit enforcement. The virtual offset of 1 is insufficient to prevent the attack for practical deposit amounts. The deployment seed mitigates only if it's always performed atomically with deployment.
deployment_assumptions: Production deployment must include an atomic seed deposit to prevent first-depositor attack
threats.config_evidence: LiquidRon_saigon.s.sol shows deposit{value: 10000 ether}() but this is a testnet script; production deployment may differ

proof_of_signal.pattern: Contract inherits ERC4626 but never overrides _decimalsOffset(); default is 0 providing virtual offset of only 1

preconditions: Vault has zero or very low total supply (attacker is first or very early depositor)
No seed deposit performed by deployer, OR vault share supply reaches 0 after all users withdraw
Attacker has WRON for both deposit and donation

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No _decimalsOffset override found in LiquidRon.sol. ERC4626 default virtual offset is 10^0 = 1, insufficient to prevent inflation attack.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on deployment including seed deposit
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: First deposit or any time totalSupply reaches 0

operational_questions: Will production deployment always include an atomic seed deposit?
Can totalSupply ever reach 0 after deployment (all users withdraw)?
What is the practical cost of the inflation attack given WRON price?
validation_requirements: Deploy without seed deposit, execute inflation attack, verify victim gets 0 shares
Test if totalSupply can be reduced to 0 through full withdrawal
Quantify attack cost vs stolen funds for various victim deposit sizes

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit 1 wei of WRON to receive 1 share (or use receive() with 1 wei native RON)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Transfer large amount of WRON directly to vault contract address (donation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim deposits — shares round down to 0 due to inflated share price
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker redeems 1 share for entire vault balance including victim's deposit
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Must be first depositor or act when totalSupply is near 0

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete theft of early depositor funds

threats.title: harvestAndDelegateRewards Inflates totalAssets by Operator Fee Portion via State Transition
threats.hypothesis_description: When harvestAndDelegateRewards is called, rewards move from 'unclaimed' (counted in getTotalRewards with fee deduction) to 'staked' (counted in getTotalStaked without fee deduction). Before the call: totalAssets includes rewards minus fee. After the call: totalAssets includes those same rewards in staked amounts WITHOUT fee deduction. Net effect: totalAssets increases by the operator fee portion (operatorFee/BIPS * rewards). This inflates share price even beyond the phantom fee issue (H002), because staked amounts are counted at full value while rewards had fees deducted.
threats.investigation_rationale: getTotalRewards() at line 238 applies `totalFees = (totalRewards * operatorFee) / BIPS` and returns `totalRewards - totalFees`. getTotalStaked() has no fee deduction. When rewards transition from unclaimed to staked via re-delegation, the fee deduction disappears from totalAssets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getTotalRewards subtracts fee portion from rewards; getTotalStaked does not. Re-delegating rewards shifts them from fee-deducted to non-fee-deducted accounting category

preconditions: Active validator delegations earning rewards
Operator calls harvestAndDelegateRewards (re-delegates rewards to validator)
operatorFee > 0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: totalAssets increases by fee portion after harvestAndDelegateRewards because staked amounts include what was previously deducted as fees from rewards
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Operator choosing harvestAndDelegateRewards triggers the inflation
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Share price inflated after every harvestAndDelegateRewards call

operational_questions: What is the cumulative totalAssets inflation per harvestAndDelegateRewards cycle?
Does this inflate share price in a way that can be extracted by timing deposits/withdrawals?
Combined with H002, what is the total accounting error per cycle?
validation_requirements: Measure totalAssets before and after harvestAndDelegateRewards
Verify the increase equals operatorFee/BIPS * rewards amount
Confirm share price increase is exploitable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Deposit at pre-harvest price to get fair shares
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: After harvestAndDelegateRewards inflates totalAssets, redeem at inflated price
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Extract the fee-portion inflation as profit

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Systematic share price inflation leading to value extraction from passive holders
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code fix to properly account for fees in staked amounts

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: totalAssets jumps correlated with harvestAndDelegateRewards calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Share price increases not backed by actual net yield

threats.title: undelegateAmount Assumes Immediate RON Return from bulkUndelegate Creating Potential Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidProxy.undelegateAmount() calculates totalUndelegated from the input _amounts array, calls bulkUndelegate, then immediately calls _depositRONTo(vault, totalUndelegated) which wraps the native RON into WRON. If the Ronin staking contract implements a cooldown period or does not return native RON immediately upon undelegation, the proxy will not have sufficient native RON balance to wrap, causing the transaction to revert. This would make undelegation impossible, potentially locking staked funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: At LiquidProxy.sol:89-93, bulkUndelegate is called then immediately _depositRONTo wraps the assumed balance. If Ronin staking uses deferred withdrawal (common in PoS systems), the RON isn't available instantly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bulkUndelegate called, then immediately _depositRONTo(vault, totalUndelegated) — assumes bulkUndelegate sends native RON synchronously to proxy

preconditions: Ronin staking contract implements cooldown/deferred withdrawal for undelegation
Operator calls undelegateAmount on a proxy with active delegations
Proxy native RON balance is less than totalUndelegated after bulkUndelegate

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: The code makes no provision for delayed undelegation — assumes synchronous RON return
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Operator cannot undelegate if staking has cooldown
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent if staking protocol has or introduces cooldown periods

operational_questions: Does the Ronin staking contract return native RON immediately on bulkUndelegate?
Are there any cooldown periods for undelegation on Ronin?
If undelegation is deferred, is there a separate claim mechanism needed?
validation_requirements: Test bulkUndelegate on Ronin staking contract — verify immediate RON return
Check Ronin documentation for undelegation cooldown periods
Verify proxy native balance after bulkUndelegate equals requested amount

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: No attacker action needed — this is a compatibility issue
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If Ronin staking changes to have cooldown, all undelegateAmount calls revert

threats.title: Permissionless pruneValidatorList Can Deflate totalAssets During Transient Zero States
threats.hypothesis_description: pruneValidatorList() has no access control and can be called by anyone. It removes validators that have zero rewards AND zero staking amounts across all proxies. If the Ronin staking contract has transient states where a validator temporarily shows zero (during undelegation processing, cooldown, or block-boundary transitions), an attacker can call pruneValidatorList at that moment to remove the validator. Once removed, that validator's stake and rewards are excluded from getTotalStaked() and getTotalRewards(), deflating totalAssets and share price. The attacker can deposit at the deflated price, and when the validator is re-added (via delegation), totalAssets recovers and the attacker profits.
threats.investigation_rationale: pruneValidatorList at line 185 has no modifier. Validators are removed based on point-in-time external queries to the Ronin staking contract, which may have transient inconsistencies.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control on pruneValidatorList; removal based on external queries that may be transiently zero

preconditions: Ronin staking has transient states where validator stake/rewards temporarily read as zero
Attacker monitors for undelegation or reward-claim events that may zero out a validator
Attacker calls pruneValidatorList during transient zero window

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Public function removes validators from accounting based on point-in-time external calls — susceptible to timing
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No operator/owner approval needed for pruning
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During validator state transitions (undelegation processing, reward claims)

operational_questions: Does the Ronin staking contract ever report 0 stake/rewards during state transitions?
How large can the totalAssets deflation be from removing a single active validator?
Can the removed validator be re-added via delegateAmount?
validation_requirements: Test pruneValidatorList timing during undelegation — check if validator is prematurely removed
Measure totalAssets impact when an active validator is pruned
Verify re-delegation restores the validator to tracking

threats.title: WithdrawalClaimed Event Emits Current Epoch Instead of Claimed Epoch
threats.hypothesis_description: In redeem(uint256 _epoch), the function declares `uint256 epoch = withdrawalEpoch` (the current global epoch) and emits `WithdrawalClaimed(msg.sender, epoch, shares, assets)` using this current epoch instead of `_epoch` (the actual epoch being redeemed). Off-chain indexers, dashboards, and analytics tools relying on this event will misattribute withdrawals to incorrect epochs, breaking accounting reconciliation and potentially affecting any off-chain systems that use epoch data for triggering actions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The variable `epoch` at line 298 shadows the parameter intent. The event at line 307 uses `epoch` (current) instead of `_epoch` (claimed). This is a confirmed code defect.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 epoch = withdrawalEpoch; ... emit WithdrawalClaimed(msg.sender, epoch, shares, assets); — should be _epoch not epoch

preconditions: User claims a finalized epoch that is not the current withdrawalEpoch
Off-chain systems monitor WithdrawalClaimed events for accounting

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Local variable `epoch` assigned from `withdrawalEpoch` instead of using parameter `_epoch` in event

operational_questions: Do any off-chain systems use the epoch field from WithdrawalClaimed events for processing?
Could incorrect epoch attribution cause double-processing or missed claims in off-chain tools?
validation_requirements: Finalize epoch 0, advance to epoch 1, claim epoch 0 — verify event emits epoch 1 instead of 0
Assess impact on any indexer or dashboard consuming these events

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack — demonstrable code defect
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Event data is incorrect for any non-current epoch redemption
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Off-chain systems may misreconcile or misattribute claims

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Always triggers for non-current epoch claims

threats.title: No Withdrawal Request Cancellation Creates Irreversible Fund Lock If Finalization Blocked
threats.hypothesis_description: When users call requestWithdrawal(), their LRON shares are transferred to the vault contract and locked in the current epoch. There is no cancel function to return shares to the user. If finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() cannot be called (due to the onlyOperator bug per H001, pause state, insufficient vault WRON, or operator unavailability), all shares locked in the current epoch are permanently stuck. Users lose their shares with no recourse. Combined with INV-ACCESS-001, if the owner accidentally sets operator[owner]=true, both operator functions AND finalization are locked, making ALL pending withdrawal requests permanently irrecoverable.
threats.investigation_rationale: requestWithdrawal at line 282 transfers shares to vault. No function exists to cancel/return shares from a non-finalized epoch. The shares can only be recovered through finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch + redeem flow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: requestWithdrawal transfers shares to vault, increments lockedSharesPerEpoch, but no cancelWithdrawal function exists anywhere in the contract

preconditions: Users have called requestWithdrawal for the current epoch
finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch cannot be called (operator bug, pause, or insufficient funds)
No code path exists to return shares from a non-finalized epoch

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Shares transferred to vault with no return path if finalization fails
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Entirely dependent on operator/owner being able to call finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any period where finalization is blocked

operational_questions: Should a cancelWithdrawal function exist for non-finalized epochs?
What is the expected SLA for epoch finalization?
How much user value could be locked if finalization is delayed?
validation_requirements: Confirm no alternative path to recover shares from non-finalized epoch
Test scenario: request withdrawal, then lock out operator functions, verify shares are stuck
Assess if owner can transfer locked shares directly (owner has no such function)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If operator functions become inaccessible (H001 or pause), finalization stops
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User shares are locked in vault with no cancellation mechanism
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Funds effectively lost until code fix/redeployment

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All withdrawal requests for current epoch permanently locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code upgrade or manual intervention — no on-chain recovery path

threats.title: Owner Setting operator[owner]=true Locks All Operator Functions Including Self
threats.hypothesis_description: Due to the inverted onlyOperator modifier logic (H001), if the owner calls updateOperator(owner, true) — a natural action to enable themselves as an operator — the modifier condition becomes: `msg.sender != owner()` is false, `operator[msg.sender]` is true, so `false || true = true` → reverts. The owner is now locked out of ALL operator functions (harvest, delegate, undelegate, redelegate, finalize). Since the owner is the ONLY entity that could previously pass the modifier, all staking operations cease entirely. Recovery requires calling updateOperator(owner, false), but this requires the owner to diagnose the issue. During the lockout window, rewards are not harvested, delegations cannot be managed, and withdrawal epochs cannot be finalized.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a specific catastrophic consequence of H001. The documentation states operators and owner should both have access. An owner self-flagging is a highly plausible action that creates a complete protocol lockout.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onlyOperator: if (msg.sender != owner() || operator[msg.sender]) revert — owner with operator[owner]=true causes (false || true) = true → revert

preconditions: Owner calls updateOperator(owner, true)
Owner then attempts any onlyOperator function

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Self-flagging as operator is a natural administrative action that creates a complete lockout
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner action directly causes the lockout; recovery also requires owner action
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From the moment operator[owner]=true until owner diagnoses and reverses

operational_questions: Is there any operational procedure that could lead the owner to flag themselves as operator?
How quickly can the owner diagnose and recover from this lockout?
What is the maximum damage during the lockout window?
validation_requirements: Set operator[owner]=true, attempt any operator function — verify revert
Verify updateOperator(owner, false) restores access
Measure impact on pending withdrawal requests during lockout

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner naturally calls updateOperator(owner, true) to enable self as operator
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All operator functions immediately start reverting for owner
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Staking operations, harvesting, and withdrawal finalization cease
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol frozen until owner realizes issue and calls updateOperator(owner, false)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Owner must self-diagnose; no external party can resolve

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete loss of staking management capability
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total protocol freeze — no harvesting, delegation, or withdrawal finalization
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low if diagnosed quickly — updateOperator(owner, false) restores access

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: updateOperator(owner, true) transaction followed by failed operator function calls
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: All operator functions reverting after operator role update
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Owner repeatedly attempting and failing operator calls
